# 35 Healthcare KPIs to Track in 2025

![Lisa Schwarz](/portal/assets/img/business-articles/author-lisa-schwarz.png) [Lisa Schwarz](/portal/resource/authors/lisa-schwarz.shtml) | Senior
Director of Global Product Marketing

![](/portal/assets/img/business-articles/erp/bnr-healthcare-kpis.jpg)

Healthcare organizations face significant challenges when delivering high-quality care to
patients, not the least of
which is navigating financial, operational, clinical, and regulatory demands. Without a
clear understanding of their
company’s strengths and weaknesses, healthcare leaders can struggle to adapt to the evolving
needs of their patients
and the broader healthcare landscape. To make the targeted and effective operational
improvements needed to overcome
these challenges, decision-makers need to take a data-driven approach to monitoring and
assessing their business.
This article explores 35 essential key performance indicators (KPIs) that provide valuable
insights into improving
efficiency, as well as patient experiences and outcomes.

## What Are Healthcare KPIs?

Healthcare KPIs are
[quantifiable measures](/portal/resource/articles/erp/key-performance-indicators-kpis.shtml)
used to evaluate a healthcare business’s performance, metrics to gain insights into facility
operations and analyze
specific strategic and operational goals. By tracking healthcare KPIs over time,
decision-makers can monitor and
analyze trends to identify successes and areas for improvement, ultimately enhancing
business operations as well as
the quality of patient care.

Healthcare KPIs encompass a wide range of metrics, such as profit margins, patient wait
times, and readmission and
infection rates. They’re often organized into distinct categories—including financial
performance, operational
efficiency, clinical quality, and patient experiences—that give healthcare professionals a
more comprehensive view
of overall strength and performance over time, while maintaining the option to zoom in for a
more granular analysis.
By setting targets for each KPI and regularly checking on progress, companies can ensure
that they’re meeting their
goals and making necessary strategic and operational adjustments as results come in.

Key Takeaways

* KPIs are specific data points that businesses use to assess their performance over time.
* In healthcare, monitoring and analyzing KPIs helps businesses improve patient outcomes
  and internal operations.
* Healthcare organizations do best when they track a balanced mix of financial,
  operational, and clinical KPIs to
  gain a comprehensive view of their performance.
* By regularly updating KPIs, companies can stay competitive and adapt to the evolving
  demands of the industry, as
  well as to the needs of their patients and communities.

## Why Is Measuring Healthcare Efficiency Important?

According to U.S. Center for Disease Control and Prevention data, 88% of office-based
physicians are using
electronic health records. But healthcare providers also track and analyze many KPIs to help
them create data-driven
strategies for improving patient care in deliberate and targeted ways. For example, say a
clinic sets a goal of
shortening the duration of an average patient office visit. To adjust processes and
workflows, the clinic can track
metrics, such as average number of patients seen daily and average patient wait time. Armed
with this information,
the clinic can begin to understand the efficiency of its processes when making decisions on
resource allocation,
quality improvement initiatives, and more. When well-managed, these improvements will lead
to better patient
outcomes, increased patient satisfaction, and reduced operational costs.

Beyond achieving improved patient care, healthcare companies that prioritize increased
efficiency are generally
better positioned to succeed than their competitors, even if market conditions change. By
demonstrating an ongoing
commitment to KPI analysis, healthcare providers can attract more patients, secure funding
from investors or
government agencies, and establish themselves as top providers in their fields. And in an
era when health services
expenditures have risen 152% over the last 20 years, according to St. Louis Fed data,
measuring and improving
efficiency is more than a best practice—it’s a necessity for long-term success and
sustainability.

## How to Develop Meaningful Healthcare KPIs

To develop meaningful healthcare KPIs, businesses must first set specific objectives and
determine which metrics
directly monitor the processes that will contribute to those targets. This may include
listing data categories, such
as financial performance, operational efficiency, clinical outcomes, and patient
satisfaction.

title: Private Equity in Behavioral Health | Behave Health
content:
# Summary: Private Equity in Behavioral Health

**Source:** Behave Health 
**Topic:** The emerging trend of private equity (PE) investment in behavioral health, including potential benefits, drawbacks, and guidance for organizations.

---

## 🔑 Key Excerpts & Facts

*   **Prevalence of Behavioral Health Disorders:** 
    > "It is estimated that one in five adults in the United States will experience a behavioral health disorder each year."
*   **The Appeal for Investors:** 
    > "The behavioral health industry has traditionally been underfunded and under-resourced, making it an attractive target for private equity investment."
*   **The Core Benefit:** 
    > "One major benefit of private equity investment in behavioral health is the ability to provide much-needed capital to organizations that may otherwise struggle to secure funding."
*   **The Primary Risk:** 
    > "Some critics argue that private equity firms may prioritize short-term financial gains over the long-term well-being of patients."

---

## 📊 Core Context & Definitions

*   **Behavioral Health:** Encompasses the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of mental health and substance abuse disorders (e.g., depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, addiction).
*   **Private Equity (PE):** Pooled capital from wealthy individuals, pension funds, and institutional investors used to acquire private companies, improve their performance, and generate investment returns.

---

## ✅ Potential Benefits of PE Investment

PE firms offer resources that can directly impact and improve behavioral health organizations:
*   **Capital Infusion:** Enables organizations to secure funding to expand services, open new clinics, acquire new equipment, and launch new programs to serve wider populations.
*   **Operational Expertise:** Brings specialized management experience in finance, operations, and marketing to struggling organizations.
*   **Improved Efficiency:** Strategic management can lead to increased operational efficiency and cost savings.

---

## ⚠️ Concerns & Drawbacks

The introduction of PE into behavioral health carries significant risks that organizations and policymakers must monitor:
*   **Prioritizing Profits Over Patients:** Risk of favoring short-term financial gains over long-term patient well-being.
*   **Harmful Cost-Cutting:** May result in reduced staffing, clinic closures, or program cuts, directly reducing patient access to care.
*   **Service Manipulation:** Potential to focus strictly on profit-generating services (like medication management) while neglecting holistic care models.
*   **Industry Consolidation:** Mergers and acquisitions could limit patient choice and restrict access to care.

---

## 🎯 Conclusion & Takeaways

Private equity in behavioral health is a complex landscape requiring careful consideration. Organizations, policymakers, and providers must balance the need for financial stability and capital with the ultimate goal of the industry:

> "It’s important to keep the focus on providing high-quality, compassionate care to patients, while also ensuring that organizations are financially stable and able to meet the needs of the communities they serve."

*Disclaimer: This information is for general purposes only. Organizations should consult qualified professionals for legal, financial, and compliance guidance.*

title: 7 Hospice Care KPIs: Track Margin, Utilization, and Cash;
content:
# 7 Critical KPIs for Scaling Hospice Care Operations

**Source:** Financial Models Lab
**Focus:** Operational efficiency, clinical outcomes, and revenue maximization for hospice care providers (e.g., "Serene Pathways Hospice").

---

## 📊 Executive Summary & Projections
Scaling hospice care requires strict management of staff capacity and aggressive cost control. The model targets rapid expansion toward 2030 staffing levels, starting with specific 2026 benchmarks:
*   **Monthly EBITDA Target:** >$84,666
*   **Year 1 Projected EBITDA:** ~$1.016 million 
*   **Months to Breakeven:** 1 Month (Jan-26)
*   **Return on Equity (ROE):** 27.93% (~$0.28 profit per $1 of equity)
*   **Initial Cash Requirement (Jan 2026):** $884,000
    *   *Includes CapEx:* $120,000 vehicle fleet, $40,000 EHR system implementation.

---

## 🎯 The 7 Core Hospice Care KPIs

### 1. Clinical Staff Utilization Rate
*   **Metric Type:** Utilization Rate
*   **Target/Benchmark:** 70% – 85% utilization *(Note: The source mentions an aggressive target of 700% for RNs and 750% for Aides in 2026, likely a typo for 70% / 75%, as the benchmark section explicitly defines the safe zone as 70-85%)*.
*   **Review Frequency:** Weekly
*   **Why it matters:** Shows how much available staff time is billed out. Labor is the biggest cost driver.
    *   *Below 70%:* Too much idle capacity, losing money.
    *   *Above 85%:* Teams are overworked, high churn/burnout risk.
*   **Formula:** `(Actual Patient Treatments Completed / Total Treatment Capacity) * 100`
    *   *Example:* 750 billable hours / 1,000 available hours = **75% Utilization**.

### 2. Gross Margin Percentage (GMP)
*   **Metric Type:** Profitability Ratio
*   **Target/Benchmark:** High-performing providers aim for >80%. *(Note: The source lists an "890%" target, but the math example clarifies this means 89.0%)*.
*   **Review Frequency:** Monthly
*   **Why it matters:** Measures core profitability before general overhead. Reflects the efficiency of your clinical delivery model.
*   **Formula:** `((Total Revenue - Medical Supplies & DME Costs) / Total Revenue) * 100`
    *   *Example:* ($1,000,000 Revenue - $110,000 DME/Supplies) / $1,000,000 = **89.0% GMP**.

### 3. Average Revenue Per Treatment Type
*   **Metric Type:** Unit Revenue Analysis
*   **Target/Benchmark:** Dependent on payer mix (Medicare). Specialized continuous care/physician time should yield significantly higher revenue than routine aide visits.
*   **Review Frequency:** Quarterly
*   **Why it matters:** Guides understanding of which specific care activities are the most financially productive per unit of time.
*   **Formula:** `Total Revenue for Service Code / Total Treatments for that Service`
    *   *Example:* $60,000 billed for Physician Consults / 120 visits = **$500 ARPT**.

### 4. Variable Cost Ratio
*   **Metric Type:** Cost Ratio
*   **Target/Benchmark:** Reduce from **60% (2026)** to **50% (2030)**.
*   **Review Frequency:** Monthly
*   **Why it matters:** Tracks non-labor expenses (travel, remote consultation fees) that move directly with service volume. 
*   **Formula:** `(Transport Costs + Telehealth Fees) / Gross Revenue`
    *   *Example:* ($35,000 Transport + $25,000 Telehealth) / $100,000 Revenue = **60% VCR**.

### 5. Administrative Labor Cost Ratio
*   **Metric Type:** Overhead Efficiency
*   **Target/Benchmark:** **8% – 15%** for established providers. (Above 20% signals fixed support structure is too heavy).
*   **Review Frequency:** Monthly
*   **Why it matters:** Measures operating leverage. This percentage *must* decrease as revenue scales.
*   **Formula:** `(Total Monthly Admin Payroll / Total Revenue) * 100`
    *   *Example:* $50,000 Admin Salaries / $400,000 Revenue = **12.5%**.

### 6. Months to Breakeven
*   **Metric Type:** Timeline Metric
*   **Target/Benchmark:** Target is 1 month (Jan-26), though realistic industry benchmarks for complex service lines are 12-24 months.
*   **Review Frequency:** Monthly
*   **Why it matters:** Shows how quickly initial capital investment pays for itself.
*   **Formula:** `Total Initial Investment / Average Monthly Net Profit`
    *   *Example:* $450,000 Startup Costs / $450,000 Monthly Profit = **1 Month**. 

### 7. Patient Average Length of Stay (ALOS)
*   **Metric Type:** Care Efficiency Metric
*   **Target/Benchmark:** 15 days to 90+ days (varies by diagnosis/payer).
*   **Review Frequency:** Monthly
*   **Why it matters:** Key indicator of referral timing and care appropriateness. Short ALOS signals patients are being admitted too late, missing revenue and quality care opportunities.

---

## 💡 Strategic Insights for Operations

### Capacity vs. Volume
You must know the true capacity limits of clinical staff (RNs and Aides). If referral volumes exceed what teams can handle, you risk burnout and quality failure. 
> "Honestly, this capacity management is the core lever in your fee-for-service model, defintely more important than just chasing volume."

# Value Creation in Behavioral Health - Executive Summary
**Source:** Circle Social Inc.

## Core Premise
Despite significant Private Equity (PE) interest in behavioral health, viable value-creation theses have proven elusive. Past successes were largely driven by fortuitous market timing and low-cost capital rather than sound operational strategies. **With over $3 billion worth of insights across varying models, we see the primary reason for this as due to a mismatch between investor theses and effective value creation.**

Below is an analysis of failed PE assumptions and viable alternative models for value creation in the behavioral health space.

---

## ❌ Flawed PE Assumptions & Myths

### 1. The Roll-Up Model & Back Office Economies of Scale
The assumption that acquiring multiple facilities creates value through shared back-office services (RCM, marketing, staffing) is false.
*   **The Patchwork Problem:** Roll-ups often result in an "ugly patchwork quilt" of disparate EMRs, operations, and protocols. **Under this model, rather than the single headache that the usual mom and pop entails, there are multiple mom and pops with compounding headaches.**
*   **RCC/Billing Economics:** Insurance reimbursement is labor-intensive. Outsourcing costs 3-6% of approved claims ($30k–$60k per $1M in reimbursements)—cheaper than hiring in-house FTEs. **There is no point at which performing RCM services in-house is cheaper unless operating with a skeleton crew, which ultimately results in missed opportunities...**
*   **Linear Scaling:** Marketing, staffing, and executive leadership all require comparable increases in investment as an organization grows. **There are rarely cost-cutting opportunities as a method of value creation in behavioral health.**

### 2. Volume Yields Better Payer Negotiations
Unlike med-surge hospitals, behavioral health providers cannot easily leverage size to demand higher rates from payers.
*   **Low Overall Spend:** Behavioral health represents only about **1% of total insurance payer reimbursements.**
*   **Lack of Leverage:** Even a 200-bed residential facility in a state with 2 million covered lives only treats about 0.1% of the payer's members. Insurance contracts are negotiated state-by-state, negating the power of national scale. 

### 3. Supply-Demand Imbalance Justifies Expansion
There is a false belief that high diagnostic rates equal high demand. 
*   **Saturated Markets:** While many meet diagnostic criteria, only a small percentage actively seek services. **New entrants simply add to the challenge of acquiring enough patients to maintain financial viability.**
*   **Geographic Limitations:** Patients won't travel far. In a 10-15 mile urban radius (approx. 100,000 people), only about 2,800 people (or 11 per day) will actively seek treatment for depression annually. With 20-30 local competitors fighting for this volume, single-location expansion is highly limited.
*   **The Copy/Paste Failure:** Taking a successful model from one city to another usually fails because **having yet one more provider that offers nothing different than established competition is not going to be able to gain market share from incumbents.**

### 4. Telehealth is the Exception
Telehealth was incorrectly modeled as a SaaS business with high margins and "hockey stick" growth.
*   **High Demand & No Moat:** It still requires linear increases in marketing/labor, and services are deeply undifferentiated. **There is no competitive moat and patients are not able to distinguish one telehealth provider from another.**

---

## ✅ Proven Value Creation Opportunities

If traditional theses fail, value creation relies on three primary strategies:

### 1. Turnarounds Through Operating Partners
Simply buying and watching a business grow is no longer viable; fixing broken operations is necessary but difficult.
*   **Deep Expertise Required:** Problems are multifaceted (poor call conversion, bad reputation, weak clinical care). **Firms can’t fix what they don’t have expertise in.**
*   **Active Involvement:** **The usual PE playbook of asking questions and making recommendations at a monthly board meeting is not going to be enough to facilitate a turnaround.**
*   **Talent Scarcity:** **Sourcing talent in behavioral health is difficult, especially at the executive level.** Firing underperformers only works if rockstar replacements are already lined up.

### 2. Synergistic Levels of Care (The Easiest Method)
A single-level provider often turns away 80% of calls due to wrong acuity or insurance. 
*   **Convert Existing Volume:** Adding levels of care (Residential, PHP, IOP, OP) allows the organization to capture and monetize existing excess call volume while generating more revenue per patient.
*   **Embrace All Payors:** Instead of rejecting low-rate payors like BCBS or Medicaid, build an operating cost structure to sustain profitability at lower rates. **Most inquiries into behavioral health providers are individuals with

title: Revealed: How Private Equity is Reshaping Veterinary Care
content:
# Revealed: How Private Equity is Reshaping Veterinary Care

**Source:** Ackerman Group | **Author:** Rich Lester | **Published:** July 10, 2025

---

## Executive Summary

Over **35 companies** are rapidly consolidating the veterinary services industry, most financed by private equity (PE) firms or family offices. This guide explains PE dynamics for practice owners considering a sale.

---

## The Evolution of Private Equity

### Historical Background
- PE originated as **Leverage Buyout (LBO) firms** in the 1980s, rebranded to "Private Equity" by early 2000s
- Notable examples: **KKR/RJR Nabisco, Blackstone/Hilton, Apollo & TPG Capital/Harrah's**
- Gained public prominence during 2012 presidential election (Mitt Romney/Bain Capital)

### Strategy Evolution
- **1980s-1990s:** "Financial engineering" — bought companies with significant debt, made incremental efficiency improvements, sold or went public in 3-7 years
- **2000s-Present:** Shift toward growth-building strategies as competition increased and valuations rose
- Current scale: **Thousands of PE firms** controlling **trillions of dollars** with **$1.45 trillion in "dry powder"** (money available to invest)

---

## Private Equity vs. Venture Capital

| Factor | Private Equity | Venture Capital |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Target Companies** | Established businesses with $3M+ profit | Startups, pre-profit/pre-revenue |
| **Stake Acquired** | Typically majority | Typically minority |
| **Investments per Fund** | 8-14 companies | 12-20 companies |
| **Risk Profile** | Lower risk, proven models | High risk, expect 50%+ failure rate |
| **Expected Returns** | Up to 2.5x initial investment | 50+x on winners (e.g., Google, Facebook) |
| **Failure Rate** | Very few complete failures | Common (e.g., Theranos, Pets.com) |
| **Use of Debt** | Significant leverage to boost equity returns | Minimal |

---

## Who Funds Private Equity?

### Major Investor Types (Limited Partners/LPs)
- **Endowments:** Colleges, universities, non-profits seeking stock market diversification
- **Public Pension Funds:** Teacher pensions, state employee pensions
- **Insurance Companies:** Allocating reserves to longer-term assets
- **Sovereign Wealth Funds:** Nations like Saudi Arabia and Dubai
- **Wealthy Individuals:** High net-worth investors seeking alternative returns

### LP Structure
LPs conduct due diligence on PE firm leadership, provide capital, but have **limited decision-making powers** — focusing on oversight and performance evaluation rather than day-to-day management.

---

## Family Offices: A Distinct Model

- Manage wealth of **one or a small group of wealthy families**
- **No external investors/LPs**
- Hold investments **10+ years** (vs. PE's 3-7 year typical exit)
- Greater flexibility for long-term gains without external pressures

---

## Types of PE Firms

### By Size/Market Segment

| Segment | Target EBITDA | Equity Investment | Debt Usage |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|------------|
| **Lower Middle Market** | $2-10M | $10-50M | Moderate |
| **Middle Market** | $20-75M | $50-300M | Common |
| **Large Firms** (KKR, Blackstone, Carlyle) | $75-500M | $300M-2B | Heavy |

### By Strategy

- **Growth Equity:** High-growth sectors (veterinary, healthcare, tech); less debt, higher equity
  - *Key strategy: **Consolidation/roll-up** — happening aggressively in veterinary*
  - Other rolled-up industries: dialysis clinics, outpatient surgery centers, car washes, physical therapy, HVAC, printers
- **Distressed Funds:** Turnarounds of financially troubled businesses
- **Traditional PE Funds:** Slower-growth businesses acquired at lower multiples; focus on margin enhancement and revenue acceleration

> *"Some traditional PE firms have also ventured into backing 'startup' veterinary consolidators... Examples include AmeriVet by Imperial Capital and Companion Pet Partners by Cortec, both of which have launched veterinary services from scratch."*

---

## How PE Firms Operate

### Revenue Model
- **Management Fees:** ~2% of total fund annually (e.g., $500M fund = $10M/year)
- **Carried Interest:** ~20% of profits (often subject to hurdle rates requiring minimum returns to LPs first)

### Governance Structure
- PE holds **majority stake**; management retains remaining ownership
- **Board composition:** 2-3 PE representatives + independent industry member + CEO + potentially another senior executive
- **Quarterly board meetings** for strategic direction and major decisions
- **Weekly Investment Committee (IC) meetings** review each individual practice acquisition

---

## PE's Impact on Veterinary Practices

### What PE Brings
- Strategic framework and capital for acquisitions
- Operational improvements and scale efficiencies
- Focus on key performance metrics and growth objectives
- **Seemingly endless resources and expertise**

# PE-Backed Healthcare Operations: KPIs That Drive Valuation

**Source:** mybcat.com | **Author:** Ankit Patel | **Read Time:** 16 min

---

## The Valuation Equation

> **Enterprise Value = EBITDA × Multiple**

PE firms have invested **over $200 billion in healthcare** over the past decade. The multiple is driven by:
- Consistent revenue and EBITDA growth
- Operational efficiency metrics
- Clinical quality indicators (reduces buyer risk)
- Market position and scalability
- Management depth and systems

### Current EBITDA Multiples (2025-2026)

| Performer Category | EBITDA Multiple | Key Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Elite platforms** | 12-15x | 85%+ occupancy, under 5% readmissions, 20%+ margins |
| **Strong performers** | 10-12x | Consistent growth, solid operations |
| **Average** | 8-10x | Stable but undifferentiated |
| **Underperformers** | 6-8x | Operational challenges, high use |

**Example:** A 50-location dental group at $10M EBITDA × 10x = $100M valuation. Improving to 12x = $120M (**$20M gain from operational excellence alone**).

---

## The PE Healthcare KPI Framework

### Category 1: Financial KPIs

#### Primary Financial KPIs

| KPI | Formula | Target | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EBITDA Margin** | EBITDA / Revenue × 100 | 18-25%+ | Core profitability measure |
| **Operating Margin** | Operating Income / Revenue × 100 | 12-18% | Operational efficiency |
| **Revenue Growth** | (Current - Prior) / Prior × 100 | 8-15% YoY | Growth trajectory |
| **Revenue per Location** | Total Revenue / Locations | Varies by specialty | Scale efficiency |
| **Revenue per Provider** | Total Revenue / FTE Providers | $400K-800K+ | Provider productivity |

#### Use and Liquidity KPIs

| KPI | Formula | Target | Risk Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Debt/EBITDA** | Total Debt / EBITDA | Under 3.5x | Above 5x = concern |
| **Interest Coverage** | EBITDA / Interest Expense | Above 3x | Under 2x = risk |
| **CFO/Total Debt** | Cash Flow from Operations / Total Debt | Above 25% | Under 15% = liquidity risk |
| **Days Cash on Hand** | Cash / (Operating Expenses / 365) | 45-90 days | Under 30 days = stress |

**Why these matter:**
- EBITDA margin directly impacts the numerator of enterprise value
- Growth rate affects buyer confidence in forward projections
- Leverage ratios impact risk assessment and purchase structure
- Liquidity metrics signal operational stability

---

### Category 2: Operational KPIs

#### Volume and Capacity KPIs

| KPI | Formula | Target | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patient Volume** | Visits or encounters per period | Growth trend | Leading indicator |
| **Capacity Utilization** | Actual Volume / Max Capacity × 100 | 75-85% | Balance efficiency vs. access |
| **Provider Utilization** | Productive Hours / Available Hours × 100 | 80-90% | Revenue driver |
| **Chair/Bed Occupancy** | Filled Units / Total Units × 100 | 80%+ | Fixed cost use |

#### Efficiency KPIs

| KPI | Formula | Target | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revenue per Encounter** | Total Revenue / Total Encounters | Specialty-specific | Pricing power |
| **Cost per Encounter** | Total Costs / Total Encounters | Declining trend | Efficiency measure |
| **Collection Rate** | Payments / Charges × 100 | 95%+ | Revenue cycle health |
| **Days in A/R** | (A/R / Revenue) × 365 | Under 35 days | Cash flow efficiency |

#### Retention and Growth KPIs

| KPI | Formula | Target | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patient Retention Rate** | Returning Patients / Total Patients × 100 | 80-90% | Revenue stability |
| **New Patient Volume** | New patients per period | Growth trend | Pipeline health |
| **Same-Store Growth** | YoY growth excluding acquisitions | 3-8% | Organic strength |
| **Patient Lifetime Value** | Average Revenue × Average Tenure | Increasing | Long-term value |

**Key insight:** Same-store growth proves organic value creation, distinguishing platforms that grow through operational excellence from those that simply roll up acquisitions.

---

### Category 3: Clinical and Quality KPIs

#### Quality Outcome KPIs

| KPI | Target | Valuation Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **30-Day Readmission Rate** | Under 5% | Value-based contract eligibility |
| **Hospital-Acquired Infection Rate** | Under 1% | Risk indicator |
| **Complication Rate** | Specialty-specific | Quality signal |
| **Mortality Rate (acute care)** | Below expected | Regulatory and reputation |

#### Patient Experience KPIs

| KPI | Target | Valuation Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Patient Satisfaction Score** | 85%+ satisfied | Retention predictor |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | 50+ | Growth predictor |
| **Online Review Rating** | 4.5+ stars | Reputation and acquisition |
| **Patient Complaint Rate** | Under 2% | Risk indicator |

#### Clinical Efficiency KPIs

| KPI | Target | Notes |

| **Average Length of Stay** | Below benchmark | Cost driver |
| **Case Mix Index** | Above 1.5 (Medicare) | Complexity/reimbursement |

# Healthcare Roll-Up Strategy: Medical Practice Consolidation

*Source: SovDoc | Guide for physicians navigating private equity consolidation*

---

## Executive Summary

Healthcare roll-ups combine multiple smaller practices into a single, larger, more valuable company through a multi-stage acquisition process. This strategy has become a **dominant force in healthcare M&A** because it generates substantial returns through economies of scale, multiple arbitrage, and operational efficiencies.

---

## The Roll-Up Framework: Three Core Components

### 1. Platform Acquisition (The Foundation)
The initial acquisition that serves as the operational and financial core for all future growth.

**Ideal Platform Characteristics:**
- **EBITDA:** $2M – $10M+
- **Management:** Strong second-tier team that operates without founder's daily oversight
- **Infrastructure:** Scalable systems (EMR, billing) already in place
- **Geography:** Desirable, high-growth region
- **Reputation:** Leading brand in local market
- **Financials:** Clean, well-organized records with stable earnings

### 2. Bolt-On Strategy (Expansion)
Acquiring smaller practices that integrate into the platform's existing infrastructure.

**Ideal Bolt-On Characteristics:**
- **EBITDA:** $500k – $2M
- **Management:** Founder-physician willing to stay on post-sale
- **Infrastructure:** Operationally sound but can benefit from integration
- **Geography:** Fills geographic gaps or adds density to platform
- **Reputation:** Strong patient relationships and referral networks

### 3. MSO Engine (Operational Backbone)
A separate Management Services Organization entity that handles all non-clinical functions:
- Billing and collections
- Human resources
- Marketing
- Compliance
- IT systems

> "This model allows physicians to maintain clinical control within their practices while the MSO drives business efficiency and growth across the entire platform."

---

## Financial Value Creation: Three Drivers

### 1. Economies of Scale
- **Bulk Purchasing Power:** Lower costs for medical supplies, equipment, and insurance
- **Centralized Operations:** Single team handles billing, collections, marketing, HR—reducing redundant overhead
- **Enhanced Payer Leverage:** Large provider groups have more power to negotiate higher reimbursement rates

### 2. Multiple Arbitrage
Larger companies are valued at higher earnings multiples because they're seen as less risky.

**Example:**
- PE firm acquires small practice: $500K EBITDA × 5x multiple = **$2.5M valuation**
- After acquiring 10 such practices: $5M EBITDA × 8x multiple = **$40M valuation**
- **Value premium:** $40M combined vs. $25M sum of individual parts

### 3. "Second Bite of the Apple"
Many PE deals include **rollover equity** where physicians roll portion of sale proceeds into ownership of the new, larger MSO. This provides:
- Significant liquidity event upfront
- Retained equity with growth potential
- Second payday when PE firm eventually sells the larger company

---

## Hot Specialties for Consolidation

Active consolidation targets include:
- **Dermatology**
- **Ophthalmology**
- **Gastroenterology**
- **Dentistry**
- **ABA Therapy**

**Why these specialties:** Market fragmentation, recurring revenue streams, and opportunities for high-margin ancillary services.

---

## Integration Challenges (The Hard Part)

> "A successful roll-up depends entirely on effective integration. This is often the hardest part of the strategy and where many initiatives fail."

**Key Challenge Areas:**
- **Technology Integration:** Harmonizing different EMRs and clinical systems
- **Cultural Alignment:** Aligning compensation models and clinical protocols while maintaining clinical autonomy
- **Financial Reporting:** Adapting to rigorous reporting including monthly closes and KPI tracking

---

## Preparation Checklist for Practice Owners

Whether selling in one year or five, these steps maximize value:

### 1. Financial Readiness
- Prepare clean, accurate financial statements
- Normalize EBITDA to reflect true profitability

### 2. Operational Excellence
- Document all workflows
- Identify and resolve inefficiencies

### 3. Growth Narrative
- Articulate future expansion potential
- Identify new service opportunities

### 4. Professional Team Assembly
- Engage experienced M&A advisors early
- Retain healthcare-specialized attorneys
- Engage accountants familiar with medical practice transactions

---

## Quick-Reference Comparison Table

| Factor | Platform Target | Bolt-On Target |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **EBITDA Size** | $2M – $10M+ | $500K – $2M |
| **Management** | Strong second-tier team | Founder willing to stay post-sale |
| **Infrastructure** | Scalable systems in place | Sound but benefits from integration |
| **Geography** | High-growth region | Fills gaps or adds density |
| **Reputation** | Leading local brand | Strong patient/referral network |

---

## Key Takeaway

> "Practice consolidation is no long

content:
# PE Due Diligence Checklist for Healthcare Practices

*Source: SovDoc | [Original Article](https://sovdoc.com/pe-due-diligence-checklist-healthcare/)*

---

## Overview

Due diligence is the most intensive phase of a PE transaction, occurring after initial presentations and Letters of Intent. This checklist serves as a strategic roadmap to validate practice value and build buyer confidence.

## Why Due Diligence Matters

A well-organized due diligence process achieves **three critical goals**:

1. **Validates Valuation** — Clean, organized data confirms financial and operational health supporting your valuation
2. **Builds Buyer Confidence** — Smooth process signals well-run practice, reduces buyer's risk perception, prevents last-minute price reduction attempts
3. **Speeds Up Timeline** — Being prepared reduces back-and-forth, accelerating time to closing

> *"Having your own advisors, including specialized legal counsel and experienced CPA firms, is essential to level the playing field."*

---

## The 5-Category Due Diligence Checklist

### 1. Financial & Corporate Health
*The bedrock of valuation; expect a formal Quality of Earnings (QoE) Analysis*

| Document/Item | Details |
|---|---|
| **Financial Statements** | Income statements, balance sheets, cash flow statements for **3-5 years** + YTD monthly statements |
| **Tax Returns** | Corporate tax returns for **last 3 years** |
| **Accounts Receivable** | Detailed A/R aging reports and write-off history (low "days in A/R" is a strong positive signal) |
| **Revenue & Payer Mix** | Breakdown by CPT code, by provider, by payer (reveals revenue streams and concentration risks) |
| **Debt & Liabilities** | Schedule of all outstanding debt, loans, lines of credit |
| **Corporate Records** | Articles of Incorporation, operating agreements, shareholder lists, board meeting minutes |

### 2. Clinical & Operational Integrity
*Proves success is repeatable and scalable*

- **Patient & Visit Data:** Monthly volumes by provider/location for **24-36 months**
- **Provider Productivity:** RVUs or other metrics per provider, benchmarked against specialty standards
- **Referral Patterns:** Top referral sources showing patient pipeline stability
- **Revenue Cycle Management:** Major payer contracts, fee schedules, denial/appeals rate analysis for **12-24 months**
- **Clinical Quality:** Outcomes reports, quality metrics (e.g., MIPS), patient satisfaction surveys

### 3. Legal & Regulatory Compliance
*In healthcare, nothing sinks a deal faster than a compliance issue*

**Required Documentation:**
- ☐ All state/federal licenses, accreditations, certifications (entity + all clinical providers)
- ☐ HIPAA, OSHA, billing compliance programs + staff training records
- ☐ Summary of all past audits (CMS, commercial payers) and pending/threatened litigation
- ☐ All malpractice claims (open and closed) against practice/providers
- ☐ Adherence documentation for **Stark Law** and **Anti-Kickback Statute**

### 4. People & Contracts

- **Employee Roster:** Complete list (W2 and 1099) with role, tenure, current compensation
- **Employment Agreements:** All signed contracts (especially physicians); scrutinize **non-compete** and **non-solicit** clauses
- **Benefit Plans:** Health, retirement, and other benefit plan summaries
- **Vendor & Lease Agreements:** Significant contracts with suppliers, IT, billing companies, real estate leases/ownership

### 5. Technology & Systems
*Assesses whether tech is a scalable asset or liability requiring investment*

- **EHR/EMR:** System details, interoperability compliance, capabilities
- **IT & Software:** Inventory of critical software, license agreements, IT support contracts
- **Data Security:** Cybersecurity protocols, data backup procedures, breach history

---

## Beyond Documents: Site Visits & Interviews

Buyers will:
- Request **site visits** to observe facilities and workflow firsthand
- Conduct **interviews** with key physicians and senior administrative staff
- Assess culture, leadership, and operational realities not visible on spreadsreads

---

## Proactive Red Flag Prevention

Address these **before** due diligence begins:

| Red Flag | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|
| High provider dependence | Well-integrated associate physicians + clear succession plans |
| Messy financials | Professional cleanup + EBITDA normalization before sale |
| Lack of compliance program | Document HIPAA and billing compliance programs |
| Single-payer reliance | Diversify payer mix |
| Undisclosed regulatory issues | Early disclosure + remediation plans |

---

## Key Takeaways

- **Start preparing early** — assembling this documentation is a significant undertaking
- **Assemble your own advisory team** — specialized legal counsel and experienced CPA firms are essential
- **Transparency is critical** — proactive disclosure prevents deal-killing surprises
- **Low "days in A/R"** is explicitly called out as a strong positive signal to buyers

title: Private Equity Healthcare Deals: 2025 in Review
content:
# Private Equity Healthcare Deals: 2025 in Review

**Source:** Private Equity Stakeholder Project (PESP) | **Published:** February 11, 2026

---

## Key Data Summary

### Overall Deal Activity
- **1,029 total PE-backed healthcare deals tracked** in 2025:
  - **151** leveraged buyouts
  - **664** add-on acquisitions
  - **214** growth investments
- Involved **420 platform companies** and **708 investment firms**
- Deal count represents a slight decrease (1.9%) from 2024, attributed to methodology changes (likely an undercount)

### Global Context
- Per Bain & Company: Healthcare PE deal **value hit record high** in 2025 globally; deal volume was **second highest on record**
- North America deal volume was essentially flat year-over-year
- PE dealmaking remained steady despite **higher interest rates and tariff environment**

### Top Subsectors by Deal Activity
| Subsector | Deals |
|-----------|-------|
| Health IT | 151 |
| Dental Care | 149 |
| Outpatient Care | 148 |
| Medtech | 117 |
| Behavioral Health | 56 |

---

## Case Study: Health IT (151 Deals)

**Most active category in 2025** (second busiest in 2024 with 140 deals). Encompasses practice management software, EHR software, and revenue cycle management (RCM).

### Key Drivers
- Federal laws (2009 HITECH Act, HIPAA) intended to modernize infrastructure have fueled massive Health IT growth
- HIPAA **does not require patient consent to sell deidentified healthcare data**
- HITECH Act mandates collection of patient data → cultivated a **multibillion-dollar patient data buying/selling industry**
- **Generative AI** is accelerating growth—AI requires massive training data, making data more valuable

### Revenue Cycle Management Focus
- Per CEPR: *"As of July 2024, 199 RCM companies were PE- or VC-owned or formerly owned, including 104 current PE-backed companies."*
- PESP identified **113 PE-backed RCM companies** (December 2024)
- **47 PE deals involving RCM companies** tracked in 2025

### Notable 2025 RCM Deals
- **InTandem Capital** → HealthFuse (January, equity investment)
- **H.I.G. Capital** → GetIx Health (April, acquisition)
- **Advent International** → PatientPoint (August, acquisition)

---

## Case Study: Dental Care (149 Deals)

**95% of dental deals were add-on acquisitions** by PE-owned platform companies. **10 of the 18** platform companies with the most deals in 2025 were dental companies.

### Growth Trends
- Health Affairs study (Aug 2024): PE affiliation with dental practices **nearly doubled** from **6.6% (2015) to 12.8% (2021)**
- Highest growth in specialties: endodontists and oral surgeons (more than doubled)

### Patient Harm Concerns
- **KFF/CBS exposé (Nov 2024):** Growing trend of dentists pulling "healthy" teeth to profit from implants costing tens of thousands of dollars
- **ClearChoice** (owned by Ares Management, American Securities, Leonard Green & Partners) allegedly used salespersons ("patient education consultants") to meet patients about implants **before they saw a dentist**

### Identified Risks
PESP's 2021 report highlighted: **overtreatment, misleading advertising, and Medicaid fraud** driven by profit-driven practices.

---

## Case Study: Outpatient Care (148 Deals)

Breakdown: **119 add-on acquisitions, 7 leveraged buyouts, 22 growth/expansion investments**

### Top Specialties
- **Dermatology** and **eye care** were busiest
- **Pediatrics saw largest growth**: at least **9 investments in 2025** (3x more than 2024)

### Expert Warning
> *"...pediatrics remains a blind spot in the private equity debate. Children are the most vulnerable patients we have, and if profit driven models expand here without careful guardrails, the consequences could be severe."*
> — Thomas Kayal, Connecticut Children's Medical Center (Becker's Hospital Review, Oct 2025)

### Cost Impact Evidence
- 2023 systematic review: PE acquisition of providers associated with **higher costs for patients and payers**
- Price/cost impacts documented in: **ophthalmology, dermatology, gastroenterology, and primary care**

### Policy Response
- **Oregon and California** passed 2025 legislation to limit investor influence in physician practices

---

## Case Study: Pharma Services (42 Deals)

Includes contract research organizations, clinical trial sites, and recruitment companies. Per PitchBook (June 2024): *"Over the past two years, pharma services has become the hottest area of PE healthcare investing."*

### Investor Attraction Factors
- Global market growth driven by pharma/biotech tailwinds
- Specialty drug market complexity incentivizing outsourced services
- Highly fragmented ecosystem → consolidation opportunities
- Aging population and chronic disease increases

### Clinical Trial Deals (21 in second half of 2025)
- **Baypine** → CenExcel Clinical Research (17 sites across 7 states) (April)
- **Thomas H. Lee Partners** → Headlands Research from KKR (23 sites across U.S./Canada) (August)
- **ObjectiveHealth** (Vitruvian Partners) → Piedmont Re

title: Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers
content:
![Private Equity Stakeholder Project PESP](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/themes/pesp/assets//src/svg/pesp_logo.svg)
![](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Surgery-suite-ASC.jpg)

# Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers

October 7, 2025

Michael Fenne

**Private equity firms are reshaping outpatient surgical care through consolidation, leveraged buyouts, and joint ventures with major health systems. This PESP report examines how those strategies impact costs, transparency, and competition across the fast-growing ambulatory surgery sector.**

[![](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-232x300.jpg)](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf)

![](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-232x300.jpg)

## [Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf)

**How Wall Street’s quiet consolidation is reshaping outpatient care**

The Private Equity Stakeholder Project’s research brief, [*Private Equity in Ambulatory Surgical Centers*](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf), examines how private equity firms have become dominant players in the $30 billion outpatient surgery market. Authored by PESP researcher **Michael Fenne**, the report traces how leveraged buyouts, joint ventures, and stealth roll-ups are transforming care delivery—and raising red flags for patients, providers, and regulators.

Ambulatory surgery centers (ASCs) now handle more than 60% of outpatient procedures in the U.S. Marketed as lower-cost, convenient alternatives to hospitals, many have instead become vehicles for Wall Street consolidation. The brief finds that private-equity-owned ASCs often **raise prices by nearly 50% within a few years**, without improving patient outcomes.

“These investments may bring many of the same risks we’ve documented across other areas of healthcare—debt burdens, short-term cost-cutting, and ownership webs that obscure accountability,” said **Michael Fenne**, senior policy researcher at PESP.

### Case Studies in Consolidation

**AmSurg / Ascension** – After a $9.9 billion leveraged buyout by KKR and subsequent bankruptcy, AmSurg was acquired by nonprofit giant Ascension for $3.9 billion—illustrating how private-equity-built networks are being absorbed by major health systems.

**Regent Surgical Health** – Backed by TowerBrook Capital and Ascension Capital, Regent forms joint-venture ASCs with nonprofits such as Mass General Brigham and the Cleveland Clinic.

**UnitedHealth / Optum** – Expanding its outpatient footprint by acquiring private-equity-assembled platforms including PE GI Solutions, OrthoAlliance, and U.S. Digestive Health.

**Surgery Partners (Bain Capital)** – The nation’s third-largest ASC operator; Bain Capital has maintained a controlling stake and sought to take the company fully private.

By the time regulators review major mergers, much of this consolidation has already taken place through smaller, unreported deals—a pattern of “stealth consolidation” that undermines competition and transparency.

### A Call for Oversight

The report concludes that stronger disclosure rules and antitrust oversight are urgently needed to monitor private equity’s expanding footprint in outpatient care. Without reform, ASCs risk becoming yet another venue where Wall Street dictates the terms of healthcare delivery—driving up costs while leaving communities with fewer choices.

[**Read the full report**](https://pestakeholder.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/PESP_Report_Ambulatory-Surgical-Centers_2025-compressed.pdf)
